elements.sent-2411-001: Gradually typed programming languages, which allow for soundly mixing static and dynamically typed programming styles, present a strong challenge for metatheorists.
elements.sent-2411-002: Even the simplest sound gradually typed languages feature at least recursion and errors, with realistic languages featuring furthermore runtime allocation of memory locations and dynamic type tags.
elements.sent-2411-003: Further, the desired metatheoretic properties of gradually typed languages have become increasingly sophisticated: validity of type-based equational reasoning as well as the relational property known as graduality.
elements.sent-2411-004: Many recent works have tackled verifying these properties, but the resulting mathematical developments are highly repetitive and tedious, with few reusable theorems persisting across different developments.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we present a new denotational semantics for gradual typing developed using guarded domain theory.
elements.sent-2411-006: Guarded domain theory combines the generality of step-indexed logical relations for modeling advanced programming features with the modularity and reusability of denotational semantics.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate the feasibility of this approach with a model of a simple gradually typed lambda calculus and prove the validity of beta-eta equality and the graduality theorem for the denotational model.
elements.sent-2411-008: This model should provide the basis for a reusable mathematical theory of gradually typed program semantics.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we have mechanized most of the core theorems of our development in Guarded Cubical Agda, a recent extension of Agda with support for the guarded recursive constructions we use.
elements.sent-2411-010: In programming language design, there is a tension between static typing and dynamic typing disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-011: With static typing, the code is type-checked at compile time, while in dynamic typing, the type checking is deferred to run-time.
elements.sent-2411-012: Both approaches have benefits and excel in different scenarios, with static typing offering compile-time assurance of a program's type safety and type-based reasoning principles that justify program optimizations, and dynamic typing allowing for rapid prototyping of a codebase without committing to fixed type signatures.
elements.sent-2411-013: Most languages choose between static or dynamic typing and as a result, programmers that initially write their code in a dynamically typed language need to rewrite some or all of their codebase in a static language if they would like to receive the benefits of static typing once their codebase has matured.
elements.sent-2411-014: Gradually typed languages \cite{siek-taha06, tobin-hochstadt06 seek to resolve this tension by allowing for both static and dynamic typing disciplines to be used in the same codebase.}
elements.sent-2411-015: These languages support smooth interoperability between statically-typed and dynamically-typed styles, allowing the programmer to begin with fully dynamically-typed code and gradually migrate portions of the codebase to a statically typed style without needing to rewrite the project in a completely different language.
elements.sent-2411-016: One of the fundamental theorems for gradually typed languages is graduality, also known as the dynamic gradual guarantee, originally defined by Siek, Vitousek, Cimini, and Boyland \cite{siek_et_al:LIPIcs:2015:5031, new-ahmed2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Informally, graduality says that migrating code from dynamic to static typing should only allow for the introduction of static or dynamic type errors, and not otherwise change the behavior of the program.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is a way to capture programmer intuition that increasing type precision corresponds to a generalized form of runtime assertions in that there are no observable behavioral changes up to the point of the first dynamic type error\footnote{once a dynamic type error is raised, in languages where the type error can be caught, program behavior may then further diverge, but this is typically not modeled in gradual calculi.. .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Fundamentally, this property comes down to the behavior of runtime type casts, which implement these generalized runtime assertions.
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, gradually typed languages should offer some of the benefits of static typing.
elements.sent-2411-021: While standard type soundness, that well-typed programs are free from runtime errors, is not compatible with runtime type errors, it is possible instead to prove that gradually typed languages validate type-based reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-022: For instance, while dynamically typed $\lambda$ calculi only satisfy $\beta$ equality for their type formers, statically typed $\lambda$ calculi additionally satisfy type-dependent $\eta$ properties that ensure that functions are determined by their behavior under application and that pattern matching on data types is safe and exhaustive.
elements.sent-2411-023: A gradually typed calculus that validates these type-dependent $\eta$ laws then provides some of the type-based reasoning that dynamic languages lack.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Denotational Semantics in Guarded Domain Theory
elements.sent-2411-024: Our goal in this work is to provide an expressive, reusable, compositional semantic framework for defining such well-behaved semantics of gradually typed programs.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our approach to achieving this goal is to provide a compositional denotational semantics, mapping types to a kind of semantic domain, terms to functions and relations such as term precision to proofs of semantic relations between the denoted functions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Since the denotational constructions are all syntax-independent, the constructions we provide may be reused for similar languages.
elements.sent-2411-027: Since it is compositional, components can be mixed and matched depending on what source language features are present.
elements.sent-2411-028: Providing a semantics for gradual typing is inherently complicated in that it involves: (1) recursion and recursive types through the presence of dynamic types, (2) effects in the form of divergence and errors (3) relational models in capturing the graduality property.
elements.sent-2411-029: Recursion and recursive types must be handled using some flavor of domain theory.
elements.sent-2411-030: Effects can be modeled using monads in the style of Moggi, or adjunctions in the style of Levy\cite{MOGGI199155,39155,levy99,levystacks .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Relational properties and their verification lead naturally to the use of reflexive graph categories or double categories\cite{dunphythesis,new-licata18 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The only prior denotational semantics for gradual typing was given by New and Licata and is based on a classical Scott-style domain theory \cite{new-licata18 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The fundamental idea is to equip $\omega$-CPOs with an additional ``error ordering'' $\ltdyn$ which models the graduality ordering, and for casts to arise from embedding-projection pairs.
elements.sent-2411-034: Then the graduality property follows as long as all language constructs can be interpreted using constructions that are monotone with respect to the error ordering.
elements.sent-2411-035: This framework has the benefit of being compositional, and was expressive enough to be extended to model dependently typed gradual typing \cite{gradualizing-cic .}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, an approach based on classical domain theory has fundamental limitations: domain theory appears incapable of modeling certain perversely recursive features of programming languages such as dynamic type tag generation and higher-order references \cite{Birkedal-Stovring-Thamsborg-2009 , which are commonplace in real-world gradually typed systems as well as gradual calculi.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Our long-term goal is to develop a denotational approach that can scale up to these advanced features, and so we must abandon classical domain theory as the foundation in order to make progress.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this work, we provide first steps towards this goal by adapting work on a simple gradually typed lambda calculus to the setting of guarded domain theory, a task that already involves nontrivial issues.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our goal is for this model to scale to dynamic type tag generation and higher-order references in future work.
elements.sent-2411-040: Guarded domain theory is the main denotational alternative to classical domain theory that can successfully model the advanced features we aim to support.
elements.sent-2411-041: While classical domain theory is based on modeling types as ordered sets with certain joins, guarded domain theory is based on an entirely different foundations, sometimes (ultra)metric spaces but more commonly as ``step-indexed sets'', i.e., objects in the topos of trees \cite{birkedal-mogelberg-schwinghammer-stovring2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Such an object consists of a family $\{X_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N }$ of sets along with restriction functions $r_n : X_{n+1 \to X_n$ for all $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Key to guarded domain theory is that there is an operator $\triangleright$ on step-indexed sets called ``later''.
elements.sent-2411-044: In terms of sequences of approximations, the later operator delays the approximation by one step.
elements.sent-2411-045: Then the crucial axiom of guarded domain theory is that any guarded domain equation $X \cong F(\triangleright X)$ has a unique solution.
elements.sent-2411-046: This allows guarded domain theory to model essentially any recursive concept, with the caveat that the recursion is guarded by a use of the later operator.
elements.sent-2411-047: While guarded domain theory can be presented analytically using ultrametric spaces or the topos of trees, in practice it is considerably simpler to work synthetically by working in a non-standard foundational system such as guarded type theory.
elements.sent-2411-048: In guarded type theory later is taken as a primitive operation on types, and we take as an axiom that guarded domain equations have a (necessarily unique) solution.
elements.sent-2411-049: The benefit of this synthetic approach is that when working in the non-standard foundation, we don't need to model an object language type as a step-indexed set, but instead simply as a set, and object-language terms can be modeled in the Kleisli category of a simple monad defined using guarded recursion.
elements.sent-2411-050: Not only does this make on-paper reasoning about guarded domain theory easier, it also enables a simpler avenue to verification in a proof assistant.
elements.sent-2411-051: Whereas formalizing analytic guarded domain theory would require a significant theory of presheaves and making sure that all constructions are functors on categories of presheaves, formalizing synthetic guarded domain theory can be done by directly adding the later modality and the guarded fixed point property axiomatically.
elements.sent-2411-052: In this paper, we work informally in a guarded type theory which is described in more detail in Section \ref{sec:guarded-type-theory .}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Adapting the New-Licata Model to the Guarded Setting
elements.sent-2411-053: Since guarded domain theory only provides solutions to guarded domain equations, there is no systematic way to convert a classical domain-theoretic semantics to a guarded one.
elements.sent-2411-054: Classical domain theory has limitations in what it can model, but it provides exact solutions to domain equations when it applies.
elements.sent-2411-055: When adapting the New-Licata approach to guarded domain theory, the presence of later in the semantics makes it intensional: unfolding the dynamic type requires an observable computational step.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, a function that pattern matches on an element of the dynamic type and then returns it unchanged is not equal to the identity function, because it ``costs'' a step to perform the pattern match.
elements.sent-2411-057: The intensional nature of guarded domain theory leads to the main departure of our semantics from the New-Licata approach.
elements.sent-2411-058: Because casts involve computational steps, the graduality property must be insensitive to the steps taken by terms.
elements.sent-2411-059: This means that the model must allow for reasoning up to weak bisimilarity, where two terms are weakly bisimilar if they differ only in their number of computational steps.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, the weak bisimilarity relation is not transitive in the guarded setting, which follows from a no-go theorem we establish (Theorem \ref{thm:no-go ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: The New-Licata proofs freely use transitivity, and we argue in Section \ref{sec:towards-relational-model that some amount of transitive reasoning is necessary for defining a syntax-independent model of gradual typing.}
elements.sent-2411-062: As a result, the lack of transitivity of weak bisimilarity presents a challenge in adapting the New-Licata model to the guarded setting.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our solution is to work with a combination of weak bisimilarity and step-sensitive reasoning, where the latter notion is transitive.
elements.sent-2411-064: To deal with the fact that casts take computational steps, we introduce the novel concept of syntactic perturbations, which are explicit synchronizations that we manipulate syntactically.
elements.sent-2411-065: The combination of step-sensitive reasoning with perturbations is transitive and is able to account for the step-insensitive nature of the graduality property.
elements.sent-2411-066: Having defined the model of gradual typing in guarded type theory, we need to show that the guarded model induces a sensible set-theoretic semantics in ordinary mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-067: We cannot hope to derive such a semantics for all types (e.g., the dynamic type), but for the subset of closed terms of base type, we should be able to extract a well-behaved semantics for which the graduality property holds.
elements.sent-2411-068: More concretely, consider a gradually typed language whose only effects are gradual type errors and divergence (errors arise from failing casts; divergence arises because we can use the dynamic type to encode untyped lambda calculus; see Section \ref{sec:GTLC ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: If we fix a result type of natural numbers, a ``big-step'' semantics is a partial function from closed programs to either natural numbers or errors: \begin{equation}-\Downarrow : \{M \,|\, \cdot \vdash M : \nat \} \rightharpoonup N \cup \{\mho\}\end{equation} where $\mho$ is notation for a runtime type error.
elements.sent-2411-070: We write $M \Downarrow n$ and $M\Downarrow \mho$ to mean this semantics is defined as a number or error, and $M\Uparrow$ to mean the semantics is undefined, representing divergence.
elements.sent-2411-071: A well-behaved semantics should then satisfy several properties.
elements.sent-2411-072: First, it should be adequate: natural number constants should evaluate to themselves: $n \Downarrow n$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Second, it should validate type based reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-074: To formalize type based reasoning, languages typically have an equational theory $M \equiv N$ specifying when two terms should be considered equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-075: Then we want to verify that the big step semantics respects this equational theory: if closed programs $M \equiv N$ are equivalent in the equational theory then they have the same semantics, $M \Downarrow n \iff N \Downarrow n$ and $M\Uparrow \iff N \Uparrow$ and $M \Downarrow \mho \iff N \Downarrow \mho$.
elements.sent-2411-076: We have to take care when defining such a big-step semantics in guarded type theory, as a naive definition of termination is incorrect: we can prove using guarded recursion that an infinite loop terminates! This is because the definition of termination inside guarded logic has a different meaning, essentially meaning that for every finite number $n$, either the program terminates in $< n$ steps or it takes $n+1$ steps, which is equivalent to a statement of progress rather than termination.
elements.sent-2411-077: For this reason, we need a kind of ``escape hatch'' in our ambient type theory to leave the guarded setting and make definitions that are interpreted as constructions in ordinary mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-078: We incorporate this by using clock-quantification \cite{atkey-mcbride2013, kristensen-mogelberg-vezzosi2022 in our type theory.}
elements.sent-2411-079: In particular, the guarded type theory in which we work has a notion of clocks that index all of our guarded constructions.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is discussed in more detail in Section \ref{sec:big-step-term-semantics .}
elements.sent-2411-081: To define the big-step semantics, we apply clock quantification to obtain an interpretation of closed programs of base type as partial functions.
elements.sent-2411-082: We describe the process in more detail in Section \ref{sec:big-step-term-semantics .}
elements.sent-2411-083: The resulting term semantics is adequate, and furthemore it validates the equational theory, since equivalent terms in the equational theory denote equal terms in the big-step semantics.
elements.sent-2411-084: Lastly, the semantics should be adequate for the graduality property.
elements.sent-2411-085: Graduality is typically axiomatized by giving an inequational theory called term precision, where $M \ltdyn N$ roughly means that $M$ and $N$ have the same type erasure and $M$ has at each point in the program a more precise/static type than $N$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Then adequacy says that if $M$ and $N$ are whole programs returning type $\nat$ and $M \ltdyn N$, then the denotations of $M$ and $N$ as big-step terms should be related in the expected way: either $M$ errors, or $M$ and $N$ have the same extensional behavior.
elements.sent-2411-087: That is, either $M\Downarrow \mho$ or $M \Downarrow n $ and $N \Downarrow n$ or $M \Uparrow $ and $N \Uparrow$\footnote{we use a slightly more complex definition of this relation in our technical development below that is classically equivalent but constructively weaker .}
elements.sent-2411-088: To prove that the semantics is adequate for graduality, we again apply clock quantification, this time to the relation that denotes the term precision ordering.
elements.sent-2411-089: We show that a term precision ordering $M \ltdyn N$ implies the corresponding ordering on the partial functions denoted by $M$ and $N$.
elements.sent-2411-090: The details of the proof are given in Section \ref{sec:adequacy .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The main contribution of this work is a compositional denotational semantics in guarded type theory for a simple gradually typed language that validates $\beta\eta$ equality and satisfies a graduality theorem.
elements.sent-2411-092: Within our semantics, the notion of syntactic perturbation is our most significant technical contribution, allowing us to successfully adapt the denotational approach of New and Licata to the guarded setting.
elements.sent-2411-093: Most of our work has further been verified in Guarded Cubical Agda \cite{veltri-vezzosi2020 , demonstrating that the semantics is readily mechanizable.}
elements.sent-2411-094: We provide an overview of the mechanization effort, and what remains to be formalized, in Section \ref{sec:mechanization .}
elements.sent-2411-095: The paper is laid out as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-096: In Section \ref{sec:GTLC we fix our input language, a fairly typical gradually typed cast calculus.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In Section \ref{sec:concrete-term-model we develop a denotational semantics in synthetic guarded domain theory for the terms of the gradual lambda calculus.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The model is adeqauate and validates the equational theory, but it does not satisfy graduality.
elements.sent-2411-099: We use this to introduce some of our main technical tools: modeling recursive types in guarded type theory and modeling effects using call-by-push-value.
elements.sent-2411-100: In Section \ref{sec:towards-relational-model we show where the New-Licata classical domain theoretic approach fails to adapt cleanly to the guarded setting and explore the difficulties of proving graduality in an intensional model.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We prove a no-go theorem about extensional, transitive relations, introduce the lock-step error ordering and weak bisimilarity relation, and motivate the need for perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-102: In Section \ref{sec:concrete-relational-model we describe the construction of the model in detail, and discuss the translation of the syntax and axioms of the gradually typed cast calculus into the model.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Lastly, we prove that the model is adequate for the graduality property.
elements.sent-2411-104: In Section \ref{sec:discussion we discuss prior work on proving graduality, the partial mechanization of our results in Agda, and future directions for denotational semantics of gradual typing.}
elements.section-2411-002: Syntactic Theory of Gradually Typed Lambda Calculus
elements.sent-2411-105: Here we give an overview of a fairly standard cast calculus for gradual typing along with its (in-)equational theory that capture our desired notion of type-based reasoning and graduality.
elements.sent-2411-106: The main departure from prior work is our explicit treatment of type precision derivations and an equational theory of those derivations.
elements.sent-2411-107: We give the basic syntax and select typing rules in Figure~\ref{fig:gtlc-syntax .}
elements.sent-2411-108: We include a dynamic type, a type of numbers, the call-by-value function type $A \ra A'$ and products.
elements.sent-2411-109: We include a syntax for type precision derivations $c : A \ltdyn A'$; the typing is given in Figure~\ref{fig:gtlc-syntax .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Any type precision derivation $c : A \ltdyn A'$ induces a pair of casts, the upcast $\upc c : A \ra A'$ and the downcast $\dnc c : A' \ra A$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The syntactic intuition is that $c$ is a proof that $A$ is ``less dynamic'' than $A'$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Semantically, $c$ denotes a relation between the denotations of $A$ and $A'$ along with coercions back and forth; the upcast is (to a first-order) a pure function while the downcast can fail.
elements.sent-2411-113: These casts are inserted automatically in an elaboration from a surface language.
elements.sent-2411-114: In this work, we are focused on semantic aspects and so elide these standard details.
elements.sent-2411-115: The syntax of precision derivations includes reflexivity $r(A)$ and transitivity $cc'$ as well as monotonicity $c \ra c'$ and $c \times c'$ that are covariant in all arguments and finally generators $\inat,\iarr,\itimes$ that correspond to the type tags of our dynamic type.
elements.sent-2411-116: We additionally impose an equational theory $c \equiv c'$ on the derivations that implies that the corresponding casts are weakly bisimilar in the semantics.
elements.sent-2411-117: We impose category axioms for the reflexivity and transitivity and functoriality for the monotonicity rules.
elements.sent-2411-118: We note the following two admissible principles: any two derivations $c,c' : A \ltdyn A'$ of the same fact are equivalent $c \equiv c'$ and for any $A$, there is a derivation $dyn(A): A \ltdyn\dyn$.
elements.sent-2411-119: That is, $\dyn$ is the ``most dynamic'' type.
elements.sent-2411-120: There is a more common set of rules for type precision where reflexivity and transitivity are admissible, and whenever $A \ltdyn A'$, there is a unique precision derivation witnessing this.
elements.sent-2411-121: These rules are shown in Section \ref{sec:appendix-gtlc-syntax in the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The reason for choosing our system rather than that one is that in our semantics, equivalent type precision derivations do not denote equal relations.
elements.sent-2411-123: Instead, they denote relations that are quasi-equivalent, i.e., if two terms are related by one then they are related also by the other up to insertion of delays (see Definition \ref{def:quasi-equivalent for the details).}
elements.sent-2411-124: However, because all type precision derivations in our system are equivalent, it is straightforward to define a translation from the more standard system of type and term precision into ours, so ultimately our graduality proofs can still be applied to the standard formulation.

elements.sent-2411-125: Next, we consider the axiomatic (in)equational reasoning principles for terms: $\beta\eta$ equality and term precision in Figure~\ref{fig:term-prec .}
elements.sent-2411-126: We include standard CBV $\beta\eta$ rules for function and product types.
elements.sent-2411-127: Next, we have term precision, an extension of type precision to terms.
elements.sent-2411-128: The form of the term precision rule is $\Delta \vdash M \ltdyn M' : c$ where $\Delta$ is a context where variables are assigned to type precision derivations.
elements.sent-2411-129: The judgment is only well formed when every use of $x : c$ for $c : A \ltdyn A'$ is used with type $A$ in $M$ and $A'$ in $M'$ and similarly the output types match $c$.
elements.sent-2411-130: We elide the congruence rules for every type constructor, e.g., if $M \ltdyn M'$ and $N \ltdyn N'$ then $M\,N \ltdyn M'\,N'$.
elements.sent-2411-131: With such congruence rules, reflexivity $M \ltdyn M$ is admissible.
elements.sent-2411-132: Transitivity, on the other hand, is intentionally not taken as a primitive rule, matching the original formulation of the dynamic gradual guarantee \cite{siek_et_al:LIPIcs:2015:5031 .}
elements.sent-2411-133: We include a rule that says that equivalent type precision derivations $c \equiv c'$ are equivalent for the purposes of term precision.
elements.sent-2411-134: Finally, we include 4 rules for reasoning about casts.
elements.sent-2411-135: Intuitively these say that the upcast is a kind of least upper bound and dually that the downcast is a greatest lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-136: As a higher-order gradually typed language, we inherently have to deal with two effects: errors and divergence.
elements.sent-2411-137: Errors arise from failing casts, e.g. casting a number to dynamic to a function $\dnc{\iarr}\upc{\inat x$.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Divergence arises because our dynamic type allows us to encode untyped lambda calculus, and so we can encode the $\Omega$ term with the help of casts $\Omega = (\lambda x:\dyn.

elements.sent-2411-139: Our goal in the remainder of this work is to develop compositional denotational semantics of types, terms, type and term precision from which we can easily extract a big step semantics that satisfies graduality and respects the equational theory of the calculus.
elements.section-2411-003: A Denotational Semantics for Types and Terms
elements.sent-2411-140: As a first step to giving a well-behaved semantics of GTLC, in this section we define a simpler semantics that validates all of our desired properties except for graduality: that is, it is adequate and validates $\beta\eta$ equality.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our relational semantics will then be a refinement of this simpler design.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the process we will introduce some of the technical tools we will use in the refined semantics: guarded type theory and call-by-push-value.
elements.sent-2411-143: While these tools can be somewhat technical we emphasize that due to the use of synthetic guarded domain theory, this initial semantics is essentially just a ``na\"\i ve'' denotational semantics of an effectful language arising from a monad on sets.
elements.sent-2411-144: But since we are working in the non-standard foundation of guarded type theory our notion of sets allows for the construction of more sophisticated sets and monads than classical logic allows.
elements.sent-2411-145: We begin with a brief overview of the background theory that we use in this work.
elements.sent-2411-146: Synthetic guarded domain theory, or SGDT for short, is an axiomatic framework in which we can reason about non-well-founded recursive constructions while abstracting away the specific details of step-indexing that we would need to track if we were working analytically.
elements.sent-2411-147: This allows us to avoid the tedious reasoning associated with traditional step-indexing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-148: We provide a brief overview here; more details can be found in \cite{birkedal-mogelberg-schwinghammer-stovring2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: SGDT offers a synthetic approach to domain theory that allows for guarded recursion to be expressed syntactically via a type constructor $\later \colon \type \to \type$ (pronounced ``later'').
elements.sent-2411-150: The use of a modality to express guarded recursion was introduced by Nakano \cite{Nakano2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Given a type $A$, the type $\later A$ represents an element of type $A$ that is available one time step later.
elements.sent-2411-152: There is an operator $\nxt : A \to\, \later A$ that ``delays'' an element available now to make it available later.
elements.sent-2411-153: We will use a tilde to denote a term of type $\later A$, e.g., $\tilde{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-154: There is a guarded fixpoint operator \begin{equation}\fix : \forall T, (\later T \to T) \to T.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-155: That is, to construct a term of type $T$, it suffices to assume that we have access to such a term ``later'' and use that to help us build a term ``now''.
elements.sent-2411-156: This operator satisfies the axiom that $\fix f = f (\nxt (\fix f))$.
elements.sent-2411-157: In particular, $\fix$ applies when type $T$ is instantiated to a proposition $P : Prop$; in that case, it corresponds to $\lob$-induction.
elements.sent-2411-158: Ticked Cubical Type Theory \cite{mogelberg-veltri2019 is an extension of Cubical Type Theory \cite{CohenCoquandHuberMortberg2017 that has an additional sort called ticks.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: A tick $t : \tick$ serves as evidence that one unit of time has passed.
elements.sent-2411-161: In Ticked Cubical Type Theory, the type $\later A$ is the type of dependent functions from ticks to $A$.
elements.sent-2411-162: The type $A$ is allowed to depend on $t$, in which case we write $\later_t A$ to emphasize the dependence.
elements.sent-2411-163: The rules for tick abstraction and application are similar to those of ordinary $\Pi$ types.
elements.sent-2411-164: A context now consists of ordinary variables $x : A$ as well as tick variables $t : \tick$.
elements.sent-2411-165: The presence of the tick variable $t$ in context $\Gamma, (t : \tick), \Gamma'$ intuitively means that the values of the variables in $\Gamma$ arrive ``first'', then one time step occurs, and then the values of the variables in $\Gamma'$ arrive.
elements.sent-2411-166: The abstraction rule for ticks states that if in context $\Gamma, t : \tick$ the term $M$ has type $A$, then in context $\Gamma$ the term $\lambda t.M$ has type $\later_t A$.
elements.sent-2411-167: Conversely, if we have a term $M$ of type $\later A$, and we have available in the context a tick $t' : \tick$, then we can apply the tick to $M$ to get a term $M[t'] : A[t'/t]$.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, there is an important restriction on when we are allowed to apply ticks: To apply $M$ to tick $t$, we require $M$ to be well-typed in the prefix of the context occurring before the tick $t$.
elements.sent-2411-169: That is, all variables mentioned in $M$ must be available before $t$.
elements.sent-2411-170: This ensures that we cannot, for example, define a term of type $\later \laterhs A \to\, \laterhs A$ via repeated tick application.
elements.sent-2411-171: For the sake of brevity, we will also write tick application as $M_t$.
elements.sent-2411-172: The constructions we carry out in this paper will take place in a guarded type theory with ticks.
elements.sent-2411-173: Our denotational model of GTLC must account for the fact that the language is call-by-value with non-trivial effects (divergence and errors).
elements.sent-2411-174: To do this, we formulate the model using the categorical structures of Levy's call-by-push-value \cite{levy99 .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Call-by-push-value is a categorical model of effects that is a refinement of Moggi's monadic semantics \cite{MOGGI199155 .}
elements.sent-2411-176: While Moggi works with a (strong) monad $T$ on a category $\mathcal V$ of pure morphisms, call-by-push-value works instead with a decomposition of such a monad into an adjunction between a category $\mathcal V$ of ``value types'' and pure morphisms and a category $\mathcal E$ of ``computation types'' and linear morphisms.
elements.sent-2411-177: The monad $T$ on $\calV$ is decomposed into a left adjoint $F : \calV \to \calE$ constructing the type $F A$ of computations that return $A$ values and and right adjoint $U : \calE \to \calV$ constructing the type $U B$ of values that are first-class suspended computations of type $B$.
elements.sent-2411-178: The monad $T$ can then be reconstituted as $T = UF$ but the main advantage is that many constructions on effectful programs naturally decompose into constructions on value/computation types.
elements.sent-2411-179: Most strikingly, the CBV function type $A \rightharpoonup A'$ decomposes into the composition of three constructions $U(A \to F A')$ where $\arr : \calV^{op \times \calE \to \calE$ is a kind of mixed kinded function type.}
elements.sent-2411-180: This decomposition considerably simplifies treatment of the CBV function type.
elements.sent-2411-181: Additionally, prior work on gradual typing semantics has shown that the casts of gradual typing are naturally formulated in terms of pairs of a pure morphism and a linear morphism, as we explain below \cite{new-licata-ahmed2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-182: For our simple denotational semantics, the category $\calV$ is simply the category $\Set$ of sets and functions.
elements.sent-2411-183: Then because GTLC is call-by-value, all types will be interpreted as objects of this category, i.e., sets.
elements.sent-2411-184: More interesting are the computation types.
elements.sent-2411-185: These will be interpreted in a category of sets equipped with algebraic\footnote{The $\theta$ structure is not algebraic in the strictest sense since it does not have finite arity.}
elements.sent-2411-186: This algebraic structure needs to model the effects present in our language, in this case these are errors as well as taking computational steps (potentially diverging by taking computational steps forever).
elements.sent-2411-187: We call these algebraic structures simple error domains to distinguish them from the error domains we define later to model graduality:.
elements.sent-2411-190: A function $\theta_B : \laterhs UB \to UB$ modeling a computational step.
elements.sent-2411-191: A homomorphism of error domains $\phi : B \multimap B'$ is a function $\phi : UB \to UB'$ that preserves $\mho$ and $\theta$, i.e., $\phi(\mho_B) = \mho_{B'}$ and $\phi(\theta_{B (\tilde{x )) = \theta_{B' (\later (\phi(\tilde{x )))$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Simple error domains and homomorphisms assemble into a category $\ErrDom$ with a forgetful functor $U : \ErrDom \to \Set$ taking the underlying set and function.
elements.sent-2411-193: Here is our first point where we utilize guarded type theory: rather than simply being a function $UB \to UB$, the ``think'' map $\theta$ takes an element later.
elements.sent-2411-194: This makes a major difference, because the structure of a think map combined with the guarded fixpoint operator allows us to define recursive elements of $UB$ in that any function $f : UB \to UB$ has a ``quasi-fixed point'' $qfix(f) = \fix(f \circ \theta_B)$ satisfying the quasi-fixed point property: \begin{equation}\qfix(f) = f(\delta_B(\qfix f))\end{equation} where $\delta_B = \theta_B \circ \nxt$ is a map we call the ``delay'' map which trivially delays an element now to be available later.
elements.sent-2411-195: As an example, we can define $\Omega_B = \qfix(\id) : UB$, the ``diverging element'' that ``thinks forever'' in that $\Omega_B = \delta_B(\Omega_B)$.
elements.sent-2411-196: We call this a ``quasi'' fixed point because it is nearly a fixed point except for the presence of the delay map $\delta_B$, which is irrelevant from an extensional point of view where we would prefer to ignore differences in the number of steps that computations take.
elements.sent-2411-197: Then to model effectful programs, we need to construct a left adjoint $\li : \Set \to \ErrDom$ to $U$ which constructs the ``free error domain'' on a set, which models an effectful CBV computation.
elements.sent-2411-198: We base the construction on the guarded lift monad described in \cite{mogelberg-paviotti2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: Here, we augment the guarded lift monad to accommodate the additional effect of failure.

elements.sent-2411-202: In other words, the free error monad is left adjoint $\li \dashv U$ to the forgetful functor, with $\eta$ the unit of the adjunction.
elements.sent-2411-203: We will write $\theta_t(\dots)$ to mean $\theta (\lambda t.. \dots)$.

elements.sent-2411-209: The final type to model is the the dynamic type $\dyn$.
elements.sent-2411-210: In the style of Scott, a classical domain theoretic model for $\dyn$ would be given by solving a domain equation \begin{equation}D \cong \Nat + (D \times D) + U(D \to \li D),\end{equation} representing the fact that dynamically typed values can be numbers, pairs or CBV functions.
elements.sent-2411-211: This equation does not have inductive or coinductive solutions due to the negative occurrence of $D$ in the domain of the function type.
elements.sent-2411-212: In classical domain theory this is avoided by instead taking the initial algebra in a category of embedding-projection pairs, by which we obtain an exact solution to this equation.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, in guarded domain theory we instead replace this domain equation with a guarded domain equation.
elements.sent-2411-214: Consider the following similar-looking equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:dyn} D \cong \Nat + (D \times D)\, + \laterhs U(D \to \li D).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-215: Since the negative occurrence of $D$ is guarded under a later, we can solve this equation by a mixture of inductive types and guarded fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-216: Specifically, consider the parameterized inductive type \begin{equation}D'\, X := \mu T. \Nat + (T \times T) + X.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-217: Then we can construct $D$ as the unique solution to the guarded domain equation \begin{equation}D \cong D'(\laterhs U(D \to \li D))\end{equation} which is a guarded equation in that all occurrences of $D$ on the right hand side are guarded by the $\laterhs$.
elements.sent-2411-218: Expanding out the guarded fixed point and least fixed point property gives us that $D$ satisfies Equation $\ref{eq:dyn}$.

elements.sent-2411-229: The Kleisli action of type constructors can be defined in any CBPV model.

elements.section-2411-004: Challenges to a Guarded Model of Graduality
elements.sent-2411-272: Now that we have seen how to model the terms without regards to graduality, we next turn to how to enhance this model to provide a compositional semantics that additionally satisfies graduality.
elements.sent-2411-273: By a compositional semantics, we mean that we want to provide a compositional interpretation of type and term precision such that we can extract the graduality relation from our model of term precision.
elements.subsection-2411-011: A first attempt: Modeling Term Precision with Posets
elements.sent-2411-274: In prior work, New and Licata model the term precision relation by equipping $\omega$-CPOs with a second ordering $\ltdyn$ they call the ``error ordering'' such that a term precision relationship at a fixed type $M \ltdyn M' : r(A)$ implies the error ordering $\sem{M \ltdyn \sem{M'}$ in the semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-275: Then the error ordering on the free error domain $\li A$ is based on the graduality property: essentially either $\sem{M}$ errors or both terms diverge or both terms terminate with values related by the ordering relation on $A$.
elements.sent-2411-276: The obvious first try at a guarded semantics that models graduality would be to similarly enhance our value types and computation types to additionally carry a poset structure.
elements.sent-2411-277: For most type formers this ordering can be defined as lifting poset structures on the subformulae; the key design choice is in the ordering on the free error domain $\li A$.
elements.sent-2411-278: The ordering on $\li A$ should model the graduality property, but how to interpret this is not so straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-279: The graduality property as stated mentions divergence, but we only work indirectly with diverging computations in the form of the think maps.
elements.sent-2411-280: The closest direct encoding of the graduality property is an ordering we call the step-insensitive error ordering, defined by guarded recursion in Figure~\ref{fig:step-insensitive-error-ordering .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Firstly, to model graduality, $\mho$ is the least element.
elements.sent-2411-282: Next, two returned values are related just when they are in the ordering $\ltdyn$ on $A$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Two thinking elements are related just when they are related later.
elements.sent-2411-284: The interesting cases are then those where one side is thinking and the other has completed to either an error or a value.
elements.sent-2411-285: Since the graduality property is extensional, i.e., oblivious to the number of steps taken, it is sensible to say that if one side has terminated and the other side is thinking, that we must require the thinking side to eventually terminate with a related behavior, which is the content of the final three cases of the definition.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Resolution: Splitting Error Ordering and Bisimilarity
elements.sent-2411-348: Given that some amount of transitive reasoning is essential for modeling type and term precision compositionally, we need to revisit our ordering relation on $\li A$ to get one that is transitive.
elements.sent-2411-349: Theorem \ref{thm:no-go tells us, however, that any non-trivial such relation must either not be a congruence with respect to $\theta$, or must not be left- and right-step-insensitive.}

elements.sent-2411-353: Thus, we choose to sacrifice left and right step-insensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-354: That is, we will define an ordering relation that requires terms to be in ``lock-step''.
elements.sent-2411-355: In order for two computations to be related in this ordering, they must have the exact same stepping behavior (unless/until the left-hand side results in an error).
elements.sent-2411-356: More formally, we define the lock-step error-ordering, with the idea being that two computations $l$ and $l'$ are related if they are in lock-step with regard to their intensional behavior, up to $l$ erroring.

elements.sent-2411-374: We call such a function that is the identity function except for the introduction of computational steps a perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-375: We formalize this property of being equivalent ``except for steps'' with a second relation on the free error domain $\li A$: weak bisimilarity, defined in Figure~\ref{fig:lock-step-error-ordering , which is parameterized by a binary relation $\bisim_A$ on $A$ \cite{mogelberg-paviotti2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-376: Two errors are bisimilar, and when both sides are $\eta$, we ensure that the underlying values are bisimilar in the underlying bisimilarity relation on $A$.
elements.sent-2411-377: When both sides are thinking, we ensure the terms are bisimilar later.
elements.sent-2411-378: Most importantly, when one side is thinking but the other terminates at $\eta x$ (i.e., one side steps), we stipulate that the $\theta$-term runs to $\eta y$ where $x$ is related to $y$.
elements.sent-2411-379: And similarly, if one side is thinking and the other errors, we ensure the thinking side eventually errors.

elements.sent-2411-382: We will then require our denotations of types to be not just posets, but posets additionally equipped with a reflexive symmetric relation $\bisim_A$.

elements.sent-2411-386: Then if our relations are quasi-representable we can in fact prove the validity of our cast rules.
elements.sent-2411-387: The remainder of the proof of graduality then is to show that this property of being quasi-representable is itself compositional: that all of the constructions we have on type precision preserve the property of being quasi-representable.
elements.sent-2411-388: While this is true for representability, it is not quite true for quasi-representability.
elements.sent-2411-389: The reason is that perturbations are not quite as well behaved as actual identity functions.
elements.sent-2411-390: To solve this final issue, we attach one final piece of information to our types $A$: a type of syntactic perturbations that represent functions bisimilar to the identity, but are presented as data so that we can perform operations such as composition and functorial actions on them.
elements.sent-2411-391: With this notion of syntactic perturbation, and additional requirements that the relations interact well with perturbations, we finally achieve our desired result: a compositional, syntax-independent proof of graduality.
elements.sent-2411-392: Next we describe in detail our compositional model of GTLC that satisfies graduality.
elements.sent-2411-393: The definitions proceed in two phases: in the first phase we define our objects, functions, relations and squares to model the error ordering and bisimilarity, and then we add the extra structure of perturbations and quasi-representability to our objects and relations to ensure that we can model the cast rules.
elements.sent-2411-394: Once these definitions are complete we show how to interpret the types, type precision, terms and term precision compositionally in our model, and finally we conclude by extracting a big step semantics from this model that satisfies the graduality theorem.
elements.subsection-2411-014: Phase One: Predomains and Error Domains
elements.sent-2411-395: As discussed in the previous section our model of types must now come with both an error ordering and a bisimilarity relation.
elements.sent-2411-396: A predomain $A$ consists of a set $A$ along with two relations:.
elements.sent-2411-399: A morphism of predomains $f : A \to A'$ is a function between the underlying sets that is both monotone (if $x \ltdyn_A x'$, then $f(x) \ltdyn_{A' f(x')$), and extensional (if $x \bisim_A x'$, then $f(x) \bisim_{A' f(x')$).}}
elements.sent-2411-400: We will generally write $A$ for both a predomain and its underlying set; if we need to emphasize the difference we will write $|A|$ for the underlying set of $A$.

elements.subsection-2411-015: Phase Two: Perturbations and Quasi-Representable Relations
elements.sent-2411-433: We now introduce the additional definitions needed to complete the construction of our model.
elements.sent-2411-434: Recall from Section \ref{sec:lock-step-and-weak-bisim that in order to establish the $\dnl$ rule for the downcast of $\iarr$, we needed to adjust the rule by inserting a ``delay'' on the right-hand side.}

elements.sent-2411-436: Moreover, the need to insert delays impacts the semantics of the cast rules for all relations, because of the functorial nature of casts.
elements.sent-2411-437: That is, the upcast at a function type $c_i \ra c_o$ involves a downcast in the domain and an upcast in the codomain.
elements.sent-2411-438: This has two consequences: first, the squares corresponding to the rules $\upl$ and $\upr$ may also require the insertion of a delay.
elements.sent-2411-439: Second, we need to be able to insert ``higher-order'' delays in a way that follows the structures of the casts.
elements.sent-2411-440: To accomplish this, we equip the predomains $A$ in our semantics with a monoid $M_A$ of syntactic perturbations, as well as a means of interpreting these syntactic perturbations as semantic perturbations on $A$, i.e., as endomorphisms that are bisimilar to the identity.
elements.sent-2411-441: We call such an object a value object, and this will serve as the final definition of a denotation of a type in our semantics.
elements.sent-2411-442: Similarly, we define computation objects to be error domains equipped with a notion of syntactic perturbation:.
elements.sent-2411-443: Likewise, a computation object consists of an error domain $B$, a monoid $M_B$ and a homomorphism of monoids $i_B \colon M_B \to \morbisimid{B}$.
elements.sent-2411-444: We define a value morphism to be simply a morphism of the underlying predomains, and a computation morphism to be a morphism of the underlying error domains.
elements.sent-2411-445: Syntactic perturbations are equipped with a monoid structure in order to support identity and composition relations on value and computation types.
elements.sent-2411-446: That is, the identity relation requires an identity perturbation and composition of relations requires a composition operation on perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-447: It might seem strange that we equip objects with a monoid of perturbations but we do not require morphisms to respect this structure in any way.
elements.sent-2411-448: A natural attempt would be defining a morphism between value objects $(A, M_A, i_A)$ and $(A', M_{A' , i_{A' )$ to be a predomain morphism $f \colon A \to A'$ along with a monoid homomorphism $h \colon M_A \to M_{A'}$ such that $f$ and $h$ satisfy a compatibility condition with respect to the interpretations $i_A$ and $i_{A'}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-449: However, this condition simply fails to hold for all terms, in particular the property is preserved by $\lambda$ abstraction.
elements.sent-2411-450: Having defined syntactic perturbations and value and computation objects, we can now make formal the final aspect of the model: the notion of quasi-representability of relations.
elements.sent-2411-451: We say that $c$ is quasi-left-representable by a predomain morphism $e_c : A \to A'$ if there are perturbations $\delle_c \in M_A$ and $\delre_c \in M_{A'}$ such that the following two squares exist: (1) $\upl$: $e_c \ltsq{c {r(A') i_{A' (\delre_c)$, and (2) $\upr$: $i_A(\delle_c) \ltsq{r(A) {c e_c$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-452: Quasi-left-representability for error domain relations is defined analogously.
elements.sent-2411-453: Observe that this weakens the previous notion of representability, since under that definition the perturbations were required to be the identity.
elements.sent-2411-454: Likewise, we define quasi-right-representability as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-455: We say that $d$ is quasi-right-representable by an error domain morphism $p_d : B' \multimap B$ if there are perturbations $\dellp_d \in M_B$ and $\delrp_d \in M_{B'}$ such that the following two squares exist: (1) $\dnl$: $p_d \ltsq{r(B') {d i_{B' (\delrp_d)$, and (2) $\dnr$: $i_B(\dellp_d) \ltsq{d {r(B) p_d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-456: Quasi-right-representability for predomain relations is defined similarly.
elements.sent-2411-457: For ordinary representability, the relation is uniquely determined by any morphism that left- or right-represents it: if two relations are left-represented by the same morphism, then they are equal.
elements.sent-2411-458: But for quasi-representability, this is no longer the case due to the presence of perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-459: Instead we have the following weaker property:.
elements.sent-2411-460: We say that $c$ and $c'$ are quasi-equivalent, written $c \qordeq c'$, if there exist syntactic perturbations $\delta^l_1, \delta^l_2 \in M_A$ and $\delta^r_1, \delta^r_2 \in M_{A'}$ such that there is a square $i_A(\delta^l_1) \ltsq{c {c' i_{A' (\delta^r_1)$ and a square $i_A(\delta^l_2) \ltsq{c' {c i_{A' (\delta^r_2)$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-482: With these concepts now defined, we can give an overview of our denotational model:.
elements.sent-2411-483: For every type $A$ we define a value type $\sem{A}$, and define $\sem{x_1:A_1,\ldots = \sem{A_1}\times\cdots$.}

elements.sent-2411-576: We give an overview of current approaches to proving graduality and discuss their limitations where appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-577: The methods of Abstracting Gradual Typing \cite{garcia-clark-tanter2016 and the formal tools of the Gradualizer \cite{cimini-siek2016 provide a way to derive from a statically-typed language a language that satisfies graduality by construction.}}
elements.sent-2411-578: The main downside to these approaches lies in their inflexibility: since the process in entirely mechanical, the language designer must adhere to the predefined framework.
elements.sent-2411-579: Many gradually typed languages do not fit into either framework, e.g., Typed Racket \cite{tobin-hochstadt06, tobin-hochstadt08 , and the semantics produced is not always the desired one.}
elements.sent-2411-580: Furthermore, while these frameworks do prove graduality of the resulting languages, they do not show the correctness of the equational theory, which is equally important to sound gradual typing.
elements.sent-2411-581: A line of work by New, Licata and Ahmed \cite{new-ahmed2018,new-licata18,new-licata-ahmed2019 develops an axiomatic account of gradual typing and proves graduality by interpreting type precision $c : A \ltdyn A'$ as an embedding-projection pair, that is, a pure embedding function $e : A \to A'$ and a possibly erroring projection $p : \li A' \multimap \li A$ such that $p \circ e = \id$ and $e \circ p \ltdyn \id$.}
elements.sent-2411-582: At first glance, our model of precision looks different: it is a relation $\sem{c : A \rel A'$ with a quasi-representability structure that gives us something close to $e$ and $p$ in an embedding-projection pair.}
elements.sent-2411-583: The relationship between these models is that if the relation were truly representable we would have that $e$ and $p$ form a Galois connection $e \circ p \ltdyn \id$ and $\id \ltdyn p \circ e$.
elements.sent-2411-584: However, we have dropped the stronger property of retraction from our analysis in this work.
elements.sent-2411-585: With true representability, the relation $c$ is uniquely determined by the embedding $e$, but since we only have quasi-representability, we need to keep the relation around explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-586: So the extra complexity of managing explicit relations is a cost of the intensional reasoning that guarded type theory introduces.
elements.sent-2411-587: The line of work by New, Licata, and Ahmed involved both denotational methods and operational logical relations approaches.
elements.sent-2411-588: On the denotational side, New and Licata \cite{new-licata18 showed that the graduality proof could be modeled using semantics in certain kinds of double categories, which are categories internal to the category of categories.}
elements.sent-2411-589: A double category extends a category with a second notion of morphism, often a notion of ``relation'' to be paired with the notion of functional morphism, as well as a notion of functional morphisms preserving relations.
elements.sent-2411-590: In gradual typing, the notion of relation models type precision and the squares model the term precision relation.
elements.sent-2411-591: The double-categorical framework serves as a foundation on which we base our model construction in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-592: However, our model is not actually a double category: the lack of transitivity of bisimilarity means that we cannot compose extensional squares horizontally.
elements.sent-2411-593: With the notion of abstract categorical model for gradual typing in hand, denotational semantics is then the work of constructing concrete models that exhibit the categorical construction.
elements.sent-2411-594: New and Licata \cite{new-licata18 present such a model using categories of $\omega$-CPOs, and this model was extended by Lennon-Bertrand, Maillard, Tabareau and Tanter \cite{gradualizing-cic to prove graduality of a gradual dependently typed calculus $CastCIC^{\mathcal G}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-595: This domain-theoretic approach meets our criterion of being a semantic framework for proving graduality, but suffers from the limitations of classical domain theory: the inability to model viciously self-referential structures such as higher-order extensible state and similar features such as runtime-extensible dynamic types.
elements.sent-2411-596: On the operational side, a series of works \cite{new-ahmed2018, new-licata-ahmed2019, new-jamner-ahmed19 developed step-indexed logical relations models of gradually typed languages.}
elements.sent-2411-597: Unlike classical domain theory, such step-indexed techniques can scale to essentially arbitrary self-referential definitions, which means they can model higher-order store and runtime-extensible dynamic types \cite{appelmcallester01,ahmed06,neis09,new-jamner-ahmed19 .}
elements.sent-2411-598: However, as is common with operational approaches, their proof developments are highly repetitive and technical, with each development formulating a logical relation from first-principles and proving many of the same tedious lemmas without reusable mathematical abstractions.
elements.sent-2411-599: This is addressed somewhat by Siek and Chen \cite{siek-chen2021 , who give a proof in Agda of graduality for an operational semantics using a guarded logic of propositions shallowly embedded in Agda.}
elements.sent-2411-600: The guarded logic simplifies the treatment of step-indexed logical relations, but the approach is still fundamentally operational, and so the main lemmas of the work are still tied to the particular operational syntactic calculus being modeled.
elements.sent-2411-601: Our work combines the benefits of both the denotational and step-indexed approaches.
elements.sent-2411-602: We take as a starting point the denotational approach of New and Licata, but we work with guarded type theory (a synthetic form of step-indexing) rather than classical domain theory.
elements.sent-2411-603: As we have seen, in the guarded setting the compositional theory of New-Licata breaks down.
elements.sent-2411-604: Our work provides a novel refinement to their theory that allows for some compositional reasoning to be recovered even in the guarded setting.
elements.sent-2411-605: Eremondi \cite{Eremondi_2023 uses guarded type theory to define a syntactic model for a gradually-typed source language with dependent types.}
elements.sent-2411-606: By working in guarded type theory, they are able to give an exact semantics to non-terminating computations in a language which is logically consistent, allowing for metatheoretic results about the source language to be proven.
elements.sent-2411-607: Similarly to our approach, they define a guarded lift monad to model potentially- nonterminating computations and use guarded recursion to model the dynamic type.
elements.sent-2411-608: However, they do not give a denotational semantics to term precision and it is unclear how to prove graduality in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-609: Their work includes a formalization of the syntactic model in Guarded Cubical Agda.
elements.subsection-2411-019: Comparison of Denotational and Operational Approaches
elements.sent-2411-610: Denotational methods have several benefits compared to operational approaches, and this carries over to the setting of gradual typing.
elements.sent-2411-611: First, denotational methods are compositional and reusable, and allow for the use of existing mathematical constructs and theorems, e.g., partial orderings, monads, etc, while operational methods tend to require a significant amount of boilerplate work to be done from scratch in each new development.
elements.sent-2411-612: As a specific example of the compositional nature of our approach, the treatment of the cast rules in our work is more compositional than in previous work using operational semantics.
elements.sent-2411-613: Recall from Section \ref{sec:towards-relational-model that the cast rules needed for the proof of graduality build in composition of type precision derivations.}
elements.sent-2411-614: Rather than proving these from scratch in the model, we are able to take as primitive simpler versions of the cast rules whose validity in the model is easier to establish.
elements.sent-2411-615: Then from these simpler rules, we derive the original ones using compositional reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-616: A further advantage of the denotational approach is that it is completely independent of any particular syntax of gradual typing.
elements.sent-2411-617: This allows for reuse across multiple languages and makes it particularly straightforward to accommodate additions to a language: adding support for a new type amounts to defining a new object and extending the dynamic type accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-618: It also may be possible to model alternative cast semantics by changing the definition of the dynamic type and some type casts.
elements.sent-2411-619: In contrast, operational methods are not as readily extensible, generally requiring adding cases to the logical relations and the inductive proofs.
elements.sent-2411-620: In a mechanized metatheory, this manifests as a ``copy-paste'' reusability rather than the true code reuse one obtains with a denotational semantics.
elements.sent-2411-621: An additional benefit to the denotational approach is that it is trivial to establish the validity of the $\beta$ and $\eta$ principles, because they are equalities in the semantics, whereas they require tedious manual proof in the operational approach.
elements.sent-2411-622: Finally, while we advocate for a denotational approach, the overall structure of our semantics could be applied to an operational logical relations proof.
elements.sent-2411-623: For example, there could be a step-indexed logical relation for strong error ordering and one for weak bisimilarity, corresponding to the fact that objects in our denotational model have an error ordering and a bisimilarity relation.
elements.sent-2411-624: Then the precision rules for casts could be similarly validated using perturbations as we did in this work.
elements.sent-2411-625: In parallel with developing the theory discussed in this paper, we have developed a partial formalization of our results in Guarded Cubical Agda \cite{veltri-vezzosi2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-626: We formalized the major components of the definition of the concrete model described in Section \ref{sec:concrete-relational-model : predomains, error domains, morphisms, relations, squares, using the guarded features to define the free error domain, the lock-step error ordering, and weak bisimilarity.}
elements.sent-2411-627: We also formalized the no-go theorem from Section \ref{sec:towards-relational-model .}
elements.sent-2411-628: Building on these definitions, we formalized the notion of semantic perturbations and push-pull structures as well as quasi-representable relations, culminating in the definition of value and computation types and relations as introduced in Section \ref{sec:concrete-relational-model .}
elements.sent-2411-629: We constructed the predomain for the dynamic type via mixed induction and guarded recursion, and defined its monoid of perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-630: We also defined the relations $\inat$, $\itimes$, and $\iarr$, and proved that they are quasi-representable.
elements.sent-2411-631: Lastly, we have formalized the big-step term semantics discussed in Section \ref{sec:big-step-term-semantics and the adequacy of the relational model discussed in Section \ref{sec:adequacy .}}

elements.subsection-2411-026: Definitions of Error Ordering and Weak Bisimilarity for the Delay Monad
elements.sent-2411-855: We define a notion of lock-step error ordering and weak bisimilarity relation for the coinductive delay monad $\delay(\mathbb{N + {\mho )$: The lock-step error ordering is defined coinductively by the following rules: \begin{mathpar \inferrule*[] { {\tnow (\inr\, 1) \ledelay d \inferrule*[] {x_1 \le_X x_2 {\tnow (\inl\, x_1) \ledelay \tnow (\inl\, x_2) \inferrule*[] {d_1 \ledelay d_2 {\tlater\, d_1 \ledelay \tlater\, d_2 \end{mathpar And we similarly define by coinduction a ``weak bisimilarity'' relation on $\delay(\mathbb{N + {\mho )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-856: This uses a relation $d \Da x_?$ between $\delay(\mathbb{N + {\mho )$ and $\mathbb{N + {\mho}$ that is defined as $d \Da n_? := \Sigma_{i \in \mathbb{N d = \tlater^i(\tnow\, n_?)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-857: Then weak bisimilarity for the delay monad is defined coinductively by the rules \begin{mathpar \inferrule*[] {n_? \bisim_{\mathbb{N + {\mho m_? {\tnow\, n_? \bisimdelay \tnow\, m_? \inferrule*[leftskip=1.5em] {d_1 \Da n_? \and n_? \bisim_{\mathbb{N + {\mho m_? {\tlater\, d_1 \bisimdelay \tnow\, m_? \inferrule*[leftskip=1.5em] {d_2 \Da m_? \and n_? \bisim_{\mathbb{N + {\mho m_? {\tnow\, n_? \bisimdelay \tlater\, d_2 \inferrule*[leftskip=1.5em] {d_1 \bisimdelay d_2 {\tlater\, d_1 \bisimdelay \tlater\, d_2 \end{mathpar.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-859: Relational properties and their verification lead naturally to the use of reflexive graph categories or double categories \cite{dunphythesis,new-licata18 .}

elements.sent-2411-863: Our presentation of type precision is non-standard.
elements.sent-2411-864: In the usual formulation, reflexivity and transitivity are admissible, and whenever $A \ltdyn A'$, there is a unique precision derivation witnessing this.
elements.sent-2411-865: These usual rules are given in Section \ref{sec:appendix-gtlc-syntax in the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-866: The reason for our presentation choice has to do with our semantics, in which type precision derivations denote relations between semantic elements of the types.
elements.sent-2411-867: In our semantics, equivalent type precision derivations do not denote equal relations, but instead they denote relations that are only quasi-equivalent, i.e., if two terms are related by one then they are related also by the other up to insertion of delays (see Definition \ref{def:quasi-equivalent for the details).}
elements.sent-2411-868: However, because all type precision derivations in our system are equivalent, it is straightforward to define a translation from the more standard system of type and term precision into ours, so ultimately our graduality proofs still apply to the standard formulation.
elements.sent-2411-869: Divergence arises because our dynamic type allows us to encode untyped lambda calculus, and so we can encode the $\Omega$ term with the help of casts: \begin{equation}\Omega = (\lambda (x:\dyn). (\dnc{\iarr} x)\, x)\, (\upc{\iarr}(\lambda (x:\dyn). (\dnc{\iarr} x)\, x)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-870: While Moggi works with a (strong) monad $T$ on a category $\mathcal V$ of pure morphisms, call-by-push-value works instead with a decomposition of such a monad into an adjunction between a category $\mathcal V$ of ``value types'' and pure morphisms and a category $\mathcal E$ of ``computation types'' and homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-871: The monad $T$ on $\calV$ is decomposed into a left adjoint $F : \calV \to \calE$ constructing the type $F A$ of computations that return $A$ values and right adjoint $U : \calE \to \calV$ constructing the type $U B$ of values that are first-class suspended computations of type $B$.
elements.sent-2411-872: The monad $T$ can then be reconstituted as $T = UF$, but the main advantage is that many constructions on effectful programs naturally decompose into constructions on value/computation types.
elements.sent-2411-873: Additionally, prior work on gradual typing semantics has shown that the casts of gradual typing are naturally formulated in terms of pairs of a pure morphism and a homomorphism, as we explain below \cite{new-licata-ahmed2019 .}
